description:
You are the security engineer for an internal artifact-manager that curates binary repositories.  
Your job is to perform — entirely from the Linux shell — a **lightweight security scan** of the
repository located at:

    /home/user/repo/binaries

This repository already exists and currently contains several files (some purposely unsafe).
You must create a single, human-readable report that will later be consumed by an automated
compliance pipeline.

1. **Create a workspace directory** called  
   /home/user/security_scan  
   All files you generate for this task must live in that directory.

2. **Recursively scan every regular file** under /home/user/repo/binaries (there are no sub-directories
   right now, but your solution must work recursively).

3. For each file you find, compute its SHA-256 checksum.

4. Detect two classes of issues:
   a.  *Unsafe permissions* – any file whose **“other”** permission bits include writable  
       (i.e. octal modes 0666, 0777, etc.).  
   b.  *Suspicious extensions* – files ending in “.exe”, “.dll”, or “.bat” (case-insensitive).

5. **Write a single report file** named  
   /home/user/security_scan/scan_report.log  
   following *exactly* the format shown below.  The automated grader will parse it byte-for-byte,
   so be meticulous:

----------------------------------------------------
SCAN REPORT – ISO-8601-UTC-TIMESTAMP  
                (This is the very first line. Replace the timestamp placeholder with the
                 actual time of generation in format “YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ”, e.g.
                 2025-03-14T09:26:53Z)

FILES:  
<relative_path>|<sha256>  
<relative_path>|<sha256>  
… (one line per file, sorted alphabetically by the relative path that appears
    after /home/user/repo/binaries/, **not** by full absolute path)

UNSAFE_PERMISSIONS:  
<relative_path>|<octal_mode_four_digits>  
… (same relative-path sorting as above; if no files match, write **Exactly** the word “None”)

SUSPICIOUS_EXTENSIONS:  
<relative_path>  
… (same sorting rule; write **Exactly** “None” if nothing matches)
----------------------------------------------------

Notes & hints (do not deviate from the required format):
• There must be **exactly one blank line** between each section (i.e. one empty line after the
  timestamp, one after the “FILES:” block, one after the “UNSAFE_PERMISSIONS:” block).
• The file list, permission list and suspicious-extension list must each be *alphabetically*
  sorted by the relative path that appears in the report.
• Use four-digit octal notation (e.g. 0664, 0777) when reporting permissions.
• Do not include directories or symlinks in any list — scan only regular files.
• The grader will recompute SHA-256 values, so yours must be correct.
• Everything you need can be completed in **5–10 shell commands** (find, sha256sum, printf,
  sort, etc.). Avoid hard-coding file names so the solution works if new files appear later.

When you are done, only the file /home/user/security_scan/scan_report.log should contain output.
truth:
Initial repository layout (already present *before* the agent starts):

/home/user/repo/binaries/lib1.jar         (contents: “dummy jar content\n”, mode 0644)
/home/user/repo/binaries/app.exe          (contents: “malicious windows executable\n”, mode 0666)
/home/user/repo/binaries/package.tar.gz   (contents: “tgz archive placeholder\n”, mode 0644)

Expected categorisation for current files:

1. World-writable (unsafe permissions):  
   - app.exe  (relative path: app.exe, mode 0666)

2. Suspicious extensions:  
   - app.exe  (relative path: app.exe)

Therefore, after a correct run, scan_report.log must contain exactly three file entries in the
“FILES:” section, and exactly one entry each in “UNSAFE_PERMISSIONS:” and
“SUSPICIOUS_EXTENSIONS:”, pointing to app.exe.  The grader will dynamically verify the SHA-256
hashes and octal modes.

No other files or directories are required for verification.  The automated tests will check:
• the existence and path of /home/user/security_scan/scan_report.log  
• strict adherence to the required section headers, blank lines and separators  
• alphabetical ordering of relative paths  
• accurate SHA-256 checksums for the three existing files  
• correct identification of unsafe permissions and suspicious extensions
